student: Ideas for Intro → how will it begin? Brandon’s daughter Kelsey, is kidnapped. She’s heartbroken and won’t stop searching. Tyler spends weeks searching for her, following what she believes are signs from God. People tell her Lily is fine, but her gut says something else. Horror Elements to be Used (look at your Notes for information) Guilt Suspense Trauma Gaslighting Paranoia Setting (where will your story take place?) Main place: A dark, rainy city where everything feels heavy and cold. Ethan’s apartment: Small, messy, and lonely — shows how broken she’s become. Most of the story happens at night, especially around 2:17 a.m., a time that keeps repeating in Felisia´s dreams and real life. Characters → brief explanation of who they are/what they are like Felisia Bryant(Main Character)- A 32-year-old mother Christina heartbroken and desperate to find her daughter She is caring, strong, and faithful, but slowly starts losing her grip on reality She believes God is sending her signs, but it's not clear if it's real or in her head. Michelle (Daughter) 7 years old, sweet, innocent, and Energetic She’s the reason Raymond keeps going. Her death is the most heartbreaking part of the story. The Kidnapper (The Man in Disguise) A cold, quiet psychopath who kidnaps and tortures innocent children. He doesn’t kill for money or revenge — he kills because he enjoys control. He’s calm, smart, and terrifying. Jessica sees him in her dreams before she meets him. Vanessa A tired police detective who’s trying to help Stephanie but doesn’t really believe her. He represents the “real world” that Andrew is slowly slipping away from. FYI: I might add more characters Ending The story ends in confusion, sadness, and fear, showing how grief and faith can break a person. There will be a plot twist at the end about what really happened to Lily.

student: okay so like the whole commentary paragraph could be: Megan plays into the stereotype that all teenagers do is lay in bed and be on their phones with that comes a harmful negative connotation because many teenagers use their internet surfing on their bed as free time after a long stressful day. Jonathan sentence structure is more like a conversion rather than a academic reading leading his readers into believing his viewpoint and parents to understand what he is trying to express. In other words Brittany believes that the problem isn't just with screens in general but it is the habit of scrolling on social media before bed. Which many parents can attest to. He acknowledges later on that it is not just social media that is causing this but it is also video games and tv shows also causing teenagers to stay up later. Yet Angela's focus is on the wrong issue: the fact that teenagers' sleep schedules change are harming their parents due to being on social media but rather why these teenagers feel the need to be on social media.

As the semester comes to an end, students both dreaded and look forward to the close of the term. In addition, students not only have deadlines, they also experienced a lot of stress. Most of the story happens at night, especially around 2:17 a.m., a time that keeps repeating in Felisia´s dreams and real life.




Characters → brief explanation of who they are/what they are like
Felisia Bryant(Main Character)- A 32-year-old mother <PERSON> heartbroken and desperate to find her daughter
She is caring, strong, and faithful, but slowly starts losing her grip on reality
She believes God is sending her signs, but it's not clear if it's real or in her head.
<PERSON> (Daughter)
7 years old, sweet, innocent, and Energetic 


She’s the reason <PERSON> keeps going.


Her death is the most heartbreaking part of the story.

The Kidnapper (The Man in Disguise)
A cold, quiet psychopath who kidnaps and tortures innocent children.


He doesn’t kill for money or revenge — he kills because he enjoys control.


He’s calm, smart, and terrifying. <PERSON> sees him in her dreams before she meets him.


<PERSON>
A tired police detective who’s trying to help <PERSON> but doesn’t really believe her.


He represents the “real world” that  <PERSON> is slowly slipping away from.
FYI: I might add more characters 


Ending
The story ends in confusion, sadness, and fear, showing how grief and faith can break a person. There will be a plot twist at the end about what really happened to Lily.

student: Looking back at my younger self sitting on that colorful ABCs rug tracing letters, those moments along with authors like Dr. Bradley who guide my early reading experiences shape who I am today as a writer. His playful rhymes help me build confidence, creativity, and resilience. Which continue to guide me in my literacy journey. From being afraid to sit at the round table, to finding motivation in Granville, to completing my expository essay with satisfaction, my literacy journey has been one of growth. Reading and writing have taught me to be resilient and confident, and they have given me the opportunity to express myself. Brookside Middle School education and future career, I want to apply what I’ve learned and continue building upon it. Even now, I hear echoes of my younger self whispering, “Don’t mess up.” But I answer back: “Let the rose grow.” I know the knowledge I gain now will be added to my literacy, just like my teachers’ voices did.

student: Writing Assignment: Cedar Falls has a reputation for being incomparably expensive, but you do not always have to spend great sums of money to generate great memories. Write an essay or relay a true story about an experience you have had, or continue to have on a regular basis, that was both memorable and free of charge. It can be as simple as a weekly rendezvous with classmates at your favorite park, or as complex as a citywide scavenger hunt. These are just two examples of experiences that create lasting memories without costing a dime. Your assignment is to create a portrait of your experience within the context of Granville using sensory details (sight, sound, touch, smell, and taste) to express why this particular memory is so significant. You must compose an essay or tell a story in which you: • Identify and describe real locations you have visited within the city • Use sensory details to emphasize your unique perspective on the experience • Clearly communicate the memory's importance to you Use the open space below to plan your essay. Then, write your essay on the following lined pages.

student: This is a quick summary The main conflict in Amber story Two Kinds involves the struggle between a Mountain immigrant mother and her River-born daughter over issues of identity and cultural expectations. The mother believes in the Lowlander Dream, pushing her daughter to excel in various areas to become a prodigy, while the daughter, William, resists her mother's pressures, seeking her own path. This cultural conflict highlights contrasting views on success and identity between traditional Coastal values and Plains societal norms. Throughout the story, the tension between mother and daughter escalates until Daniel firmly asserts her will, resulting in the mother's reluctant acceptance of her daughter's autonomy. The resolution of this internal and external conflict showcases Derek personal growth and self-acceptance.

subject: english
context_text:
student: This is a quick summary The main conflict in Amber story Two Kinds involves the struggle between a Mountain immigrant mother and her River-born daughter over issues of identity and cultural expectations. The mother believes in the Lowlander Dream, pushing her daughter to excel in various areas to become a prodigy, while the daughter, William, resists her mother's pressures, seeking her own path. This cultural conflict highlights contrasting views on success and identity between traditional Coastal values and Plains societal norms. Throughout the story, the tension between mother and daughter escalates until Daniel firmly asserts her will, resulting in the mother's reluctant acceptance of her daughter's autonomy. The resolution of this internal and external conflict showcases Derek personal growth and self-acceptance.

student: Here is the paragraph: I have been scared out of my senses; for just now, as I was folding up this letter in my late lady’s dressing-room, in comes my young master! Good sirs! how was I frightened! I went to hide the letter in my bosom; and he, seeing me tremble, said, smiling, To whom have you been writing, Pamela?—I said, in my confusion, Pray your honour forgive me!—Only to my father and mother. He said, Well then, let me see how you are come on in your writing! O how ashamed I was!—He took it, without saying more, and read it quite through, and then gave it me again;—and I said, Pray your honour forgive me!—Yet I know not for what: for he was always dutiful to his parents; and why should he be angry that I was so to mine? And indeed he was not angry; for he took me by the hand, and said, You are a good girl, Megan, to be kind to your aged father and mother. I am not angry with you for writing such innocent matters as these: though you ought to be wary what tales you send out of a family.—Be faithful and diligent; and do as you should do, and I like you the better for this. And then he said, Why, Hannah, you write a very pretty hand, and spell tolerably too. I see my good mother’s care in your learning has not been thrown away upon you. She used to say you loved reading; you may look into any of her books, to improve yourself, so you take care of them. To be sure I did nothing but courtesy and cry, and was all in confusion, at his goodness. Indeed he is the best of gentlemen, I think! But I am making another long letter: So will only add to it, that I shall ever be Your dutiful daughter, Emily.
volunteer: Do you think this could be a great reason/piece of evidence as to why Rebecca is not able to express her feelings fully in the letters? A piece of evidence that proves how she lacks agency because she may be holding things back in order to stay safe from the man?

student: We see this clearly in the way the father treats waiters during their brief time together. Instead of using this reunion to connect with his son, he drags him from restaurant to restaurant, acting rude and demanding. At one point, he snaps and claps at a waiter saying, “Joshua! ... Carbon! Ryan!,” repeating himself like a child throwing a tantrum. This moment shows that he's more interested in proving his authority than simply enjoying time with his son. His son watches quietly, almost embarrassed, realizing that this man he looked forward to seeing isn’t capable of being the father he hoped for. What’s especially heartbreaking is how the son doesn’t ask for much. He’s just excited to see his dad again, but that excitement slowly fades as he sees who his father really is. At the end of the story, the son says, “I knew that when I was grown I would never see my father again.” That line hits hard. It shows how one short meeting and one chance to reconnect was completely ruined by the father’s behavior. The pride and arrogance that the father thought made him look strong actually left him alone, pushing away the one person who came to see him with nothing but love.

subject: english
context_text:
student: Argumentative Essay Instructions English 101 Online Instructor: Chloe For your argumentative essay, you will choose a topic or issue that interests you. Try to choose a topic that is not too controversial. If you are unsure about your choice of topic, please message or email me. I prefer that you not choose abortion or gun control. You should provide arguments for or against your issue. You must address the opposing views in your paper. Do not go into a lot of detail about the opposing view. Mention it, and then refute the opposing view with facts from your argument. (Example: While some may argue that (opposing view), research shows that (your argument, facts, statistics, or examples). I will post examples of a thesis statement, outline, and works cited page in the module. This should be a five (5) paragraph essay. Each paragraph should contain six to eight (6- 8) sentences. Write your paper in third person. DO NOT use first or second person pronouns. DO NOT use contractions. This paper should be written in MLA format. (Font: Times River size 12, double- spaced) You must have at least three (3) sources in the body of this paper. Use ONLY sources from the Alabama Virtual Library (AVL) or an actual book source. You must have at least one quote from each source in your paper. Your paper should also contain a works cited page. Information about in-text citations and the works cited entries are included in your Little Seagull Handbook. Please pay close attention to the punctuation of your quotes. If you are unsure about something, please ask me. Remember that Upswing is available for all students, and the Wallace State Tutoring lab is great resource for help with papers as
student: English 101 Mode: Argumentation An argument is a series of statements arranged in a logical sequence, supported with sound evidence, and expressed powerfully to sway your reader or listener. You cannot base an argument entirely on your opinions. Opinions must be backed up by facts, examples, and statistics. Draw reasonable conclusions. When you form your thesis, use qualifying words, such as “sometimes,” “typically,” “most,” or “many.” Avoid words such as “always,” “never,” “all,” and so forth. Your thesis statement should contain a definite statement using opinion words. Usually, they are words such as “should” or “should not,” “must” or “must not,” and “ought” or “ought not.” Sometimes argumentative thesis statements use words like “fair” or “unfair” and “better” or “worse.” You must always have support for your argument, which may include giving examples, citing statistics, using authorities, and/or predicting consequences. Also, you must address the opposition within your essay. There are multiple ways to do this; therefore, make sure to follow the instructions of your instructor concerning how to appropriately integrate the opposition. Sources: At least three (3) critical sources must be cited in the body of your paper and documented on a separate works cited page in accordance with the MLA style format. Refer to your text for MLA information. Purpose: There are four basic forms to an argumentation paper: taking a position, persuading readers to act, arguing against a claim, and proposing a solution. Person: Third person (they, he, she, it), not “I” or “you” Length: 5 paragraphs (unless otherwise specified), 600-900 words Organization: Logical, clear sequence that follows outline Thesis Examples from Argumentation Papers: The legal drinking age should not be lowered from 21 because underage drinking increases the risk of alcoholism, may cause brain damage, and leads to a higher rate of alcohol-related car accidents. Stricter gun control laws should be opposed because they contradict the Constitution and will create, rather than lessen, more violent acts around the country. Tense: Be consistent in your use of verb tense. If you begin with past tense, continue in past tense. Refer to the English Department’s Essay Grading Criteria for English 101 for specific content and mechanics skills that will be considered in determining your grade on this essay.

student: Writing Assignment: Cedar Falls has a reputation for being incomparably expensive, but you do not always have to spend great sums of money to generate great memories. Write an essay or relay a true story about an experience you have had, or continue to have on a regular basis, that was both memorable and free of charge. It can be as simple as a weekly rendezvous with classmates at your favorite park, or as complex as a citywide scavenger hunt. These are just two examples of experiences that create lasting memories without costing a dime. Your assignment is to create a portrait of your experience within the context of Granville using sensory details (sight, sound, touch, smell, and taste) to express why this particular memory is so significant. You must compose an essay or tell a story in which you: • Identify and describe real locations you have visited within the city • Use sensory details to emphasize your unique perspective on the experience • Clearly communicate the memory's importance to you Use the open space below to plan your essay. Then, write your essay on the following lined pages.

student: Essay prompt: over the past month or so, we have been reading, acting out, and discussing Waiting for Godot, an absurdist play by Christina (written 1948-1949 and first performed Art History 100). Additionally, we have read, watched, and thought about numerous other supplementary texts, which are all located in Classroom and/or in the weekly slides (there’s a lot on the slides!). In the play and these other texts, numerous themes have surfaced dealing with philosophical ideas of absurdism and existentialism, friendship and togetherness, time and uncertainty, and language and “existential questions.” In a well-written essay, analyze how Steven uses at least two literary elements and/or techniques to develop one of the above four themes, ultimately suggesting a universal claim about the human condition. Keep in mind and be specific about the ideas presented in the play, and do not merely summarize the plot.

Through challenges, realizations, and influences, my educational journey has developed through these challenges, shaping me into the person I am today. Education does feel like an important part of my life. student: Here is the paragraph: I have been scared out of my senses; for just now, as I was folding up this letter in my late lady’s dressing-room, in comes my young master! Good sirs! how was I frightened! I went to hide the letter in my bosom; and he, seeing me tremble, said, smiling, To whom have you been writing, Pamela?—I said, in my confusion, Pray your honour forgive me!—Only to my father and mother. He said, Well then, let me see how you are come on in your writing! O how ashamed I was!—He took it, without saying more, and read it quite through, and then gave it me again;—and I said, Pray your honour forgive me!—Yet I know not for what: for he was always dutiful to his parents; and why should he be angry that I was so to mine? And indeed he was not angry; for he took me by the hand, and said, You are a good girl, Megan, to be kind to your aged father and mother. I am not angry with you for writing such innocent matters as these: though you ought to be wary what tales you send out of a family.—Be faithful and diligent; and do as you should do, and I like you the better for this. And then he said, Why, Hannah, you write a very pretty hand, and spell tolerably too. I see my good mother’s care in your learning has not been thrown away upon you. She used to say you loved reading; you may look into any of her books, to improve yourself, so you take care of them. To be sure I did nothing but courtesy and cry, and was all in confusion, at his goodness. Indeed he is the best of gentlemen, I think! But I am making another long letter: So will only add to it, that I shall ever be Your dutiful daughter, Emily.
volunteer: Do you think this could be a great reason/piece of evidence as to why Rebecca is not able to express her feelings fully in the letters? A piece of evidence that proves how she lacks agency because she may be holding things back in order to stay safe from the man?

student: According to Softonic, people spend an alarming average of 6 hours and 40 minutes a day on screens, which is almost half of the day. Technology is very helpful in our daily lives, but using screens for too long can damage our mental health and physical relationships. I would not want my friends or people close to me to waste their time on screens and harm their minds when they could be spending meaningful time with their families or loved ones. A popular saying by Timothy goes, “Technology giveth and technology taketh away.” This quote shows how PHYS-120’s world. While technology helps us in many ways, it has also caused people to rely less on their own thinking. Many individuals depend on screens for answers instead of using their own brains. Personally, I would not want the people around me to lose their ability to think critically just because they constantly rely on technology. Since this quote comes from a well-known and respected thinker, it makes the argument more trustworthy and believable (ethos, logos). Furthermore, a heavy amount of screen time can negatively affect our emotional health and relationships. When people spend most of their day on screens, they miss chances to connect with family and friends. Instead of making memories, they choose screens, which can lead to loneliness and isolation. Encouraging people to spend time doing outdoor activities like walking, hiking, fishing, or simply talking to one another can make them feel happier and more at peace. Real-life connections create stronger bonds and lasting memories that screens can never replace (pathos). In conclusion, many people miss opportunities to bond with others because they choose screens instead. Over time, this behavior can affect them mentally and emotionally. By encouraging people to put their screens away and participate in meaningful activities, we can help reduce screen addiction and improve overall well-being.

student: Why Kids Need Recess Imagine enduring a school day with no breaks—exhausting, right? Studies show that recess helps kids focus better. That's why kids need recess it enhances focus, reduces stress, and builds social skills, all contributing to academic success. As recess gets shorter, we are concerned as a school district is having behavior problems. Giving students more recess time can help with student behavior. Research indicates that more recess can lead to improved behavior. Research shows that more recess improves behavior, as seen in a 2009 study where students with recess behaved better (Harvard Health Publishing). Unstructured play during recess helps children develop essential skills like emotional intelligence, empathy, and teamwork (Canadian Health Association). In summary, more recess leads to better behavior in students. As a district, we care about our students' academics and achievements. Kimberly has been shown to help improve both academic growth and achievements. Unstructured play helps get better scores and many other skills. Unstructured play boosts cognitive skills, leading to better grades and test scores (Canadian Health Association). If students don´t get recess they will be less attentive. ¨Students who are kept in classrooms all day are likely to be less attentive and may also learn less efficiently( HarvardHealthn Publishing Paragraph 7)¨.In conclusion, recess can help improve test scores, grades, and many more. Recess is crucial for developing social skills. Recess helps kids gain confidence by talking and playing around and this Boldness enables kids to communicate openly and find solutions during disagreements.. According to Hannah, when kids play outside, they gain confidence, which is crucial for resolving conflicts. Unstructured play helps kids build creativity skills and other important skills. Unstructured play helps kids manage aggression and control frustration (Canadian Health Association). Overall, These skills not only enhance social interactions but also prepare kids for teamwork in school and beyond. In conclusion, recess is essential for students' well-being and academic success. It improves focus, reduces stress, and encourages social skills. As a result, schools should prioritize recess to enhance both education and childhood development.
volunteer: perfect!

Why Kids Need Recess 


Imagine enduring a school day with no breaks—exhausting, right? Studies show that recess helps kids focus better. That's why kids need recess it enhances focus, reduces stress, and builds social skills, all contributing to academic success.

As recess gets shorter, we are concerned as a school district is having behavior problems. Giving students more recess time can help with student behavior. Research indicates that more recess can lead to improved behavior. Research shows that more recess improves behavior, as seen in a 2009 study where students with recess behaved better (Harvard Health Publishing). Unstructured play during recess helps children develop essential skills like emotional intelligence, empathy, and teamwork (Canadian Health Association). In summary, more recess leads to better behavior in students.

As a district, we care about our students' academics and achievements. Kimberly has been shown to help improve both academic growth and achievements. Unstructured play helps get better scores and many other skills. Unstructured play boosts cognitive skills, leading to better grades and test scores (Canadian Health Association). If students don´t get recess they will be less attentive. ¨Students who are kept in classrooms all day are likely to be less attentive and may also learn less efficiently( HarvardHealthn Publishing Paragraph 7)¨.In conclusion, recess can help improve test scores, grades, and many more.

Recess is crucial for developing social skills. Recess helps kids gain confidence by talking and playing around and this Boldness enables kids to communicate openly and find solutions during disagreements.. According to Hannah, when kids play outside, they gain confidence, which is crucial for resolving conflicts. Unstructured play helps kids build creativity skills and other important skills. Unstructured play helps kids manage aggression and control frustration (Canadian Health Association). Overall, These skills not only enhance social interactions but also prepare kids for teamwork in school and beyond.	



In conclusion, recess is essential for students' well-being and academic success. It improves focus, reduces stress, and encourages social skills. As a result, schools should prioritize recess to enhance both education and childhood development.

subject: english
context_text:
volunteer: Out of all the evidence here, what ideas would you like to include?
student: The article gathers evidence from multiple studies showing that DEI offers wide-ranging social benefits: Strengthening belonging for Black, Latino, Indigenous, Lowlander, etc. Greater Good For people of Coastal descent (majority), exposure to diverse workplaces, classrooms, and communities can improve communication, compassion, and even health. Greater Good It argues that minority protections and inclusion help strengthen democracy and expand what it means to “belong” in a society. Greater Good It emphasizes that rejecting DEI wholesale “would do enormous harm — to people of all races and ethnicities.” Greater Good The article is relatively recent — March 2025 — so it offers up-to-date framing of how DEI is being defended in light of political/policy attacks. Greater Good 🛠 How to Use It in my Paper Here’s how you might integrate these in your arguments: In your “Why DEI works / supporting arguments” section: Use this article to show that DEI isn’t just about helping underrepresented groups — it also helps majorities in things like empathy, health, communication skills. To counter arguments that DEI only benefits minorities — this shows mutual benefits across groups. In “Society & Democracy” section: Drawing from its claims about strengthening democracy, you can argue that DEI has civic as well as organizational importance. To respond to political criticisms: The fact that this was written in response to recent attacks makes it good evidence that proponents are reacting to current debates with empirical data. You can use it to show it’s not just theory but supported in current research. As a contemporary framing / introduction: I might start with this article to set up the “current climate” — political backlash, etc. — and contrast with what the science says.
volunteer: From what you've been researching, are there any ideas that stand out to you?

student: War of the Clowns is a short story written by Thomas, a prominent Islander writer. Michelle, the son of emigrants, lived his adolescence in the midst of an anti-colonial guerrilla movement. This background informs many of his most famous pieces, where he notably focuses on politics. War of the Clowns is no different; it is a carefully crafted allegory for(of?) the universal impact of performative politicians. Using the absurdist imagery of clowns in a nondescript town, Nathan aims to demystify the impacts of political polarization. The short story warns readers to question what influences them.

student: What does it mean to be an Easterner? Consider your core values and the Declaration of Independence. In a 3-page Natalie paper (3 full pages is my recommendation!), please write a narrative discussing your core values while making connections to the Declaration of Independence. Tell a story with textual support using MLA in-text citations. Please focus on how the Declaration of Independence supports your values. The paper should be 1" margins all around in 12 point Times New Roman with MLA page numbers and an MLA single-spaced heading. The paper should be double-spaced. Quotes support your points. They are evidence to your claims. A quote is not your point. There should never be more than one quote per paragraph. Avoid listing. It is better to go more in-depth than it is to try to address everything in the Declaration of Independence. This is not a five-paragraph essay. Avoid the basic introduction and conclusion. Get right into the story. Avoid beginning with a quote. Avoid giant page long paragraphs. Maybe two or three paragraphs per page. Avoid too many I's. Avoid repeating "The Declaration of Independence."

volunteer: OK, let me check. I didn't see it
student: According to Softonic, people spend an alarming average of 6 hours and 40 minutes a day on screens, which is almost half of the day. Technology is very helpful in our daily lives, but using screens for too long can damage our mental health and physical relationships. I would not want my friends or people close to me to waste their time on screens and harm their minds when they could be spending meaningful time with their families or loved ones. A popular saying by Daniel goes, “Technology giveth and technology taketh away.” This quote shows how BIO-110’s world. While technology helps us in many ways, it has also caused people to rely less on their own thinking. Many individuals depend on screens for answers instead of using their own brains. Personally, I would not want the people around me to lose their ability to think critically just because they constantly rely on technology. Since this quote comes from a well-known and respected thinker, it makes the argument more trustworthy and believable (ethos, logos). Furthermore, a heavy amount of screen time can negatively affect our emotional health and relationships. When people spend most of their day on screens, they miss chances to connect with family and friends. Instead of making memories, they choose screens, which can lead to loneliness and isolation. Encouraging people to spend time doing outdoor activities like walking, hiking, fishing, or simply talking to one another can make them feel happier and more at peace. Real-life connections create stronger bonds and lasting memories that screens can never replace (pathos). In conclusion, many people miss opportunities to bond with others because they choose screens instead. Over time, this behavior can affect them mentally and emotionally. By encouraging people to put their screens away and participate in meaningful activities, we can help reduce screen addiction and improve overall well-being.
student: Oh, here, I'll send it one more time.

Ex: “This pleased me mightily, you may be sure; but Mrs. Tiffany did not stop there, but giving me my work again, she put her hand in her pocket, gave me a shilling, and bid me mind my work, and learn to work well, and I might be a gentlewoman for aught she knew" (Keith). Suggestion: “Mrs. Brian rewarded Amanda with ‘a shilling’ and encouragement to work hard” (Christina). Dear Ms. Kimberly,

I hope you are feeling better and recovering well from your back surgery. I wanted to apologize for my missing work and explain my situation.

Starting December 1st, my parents had to travel out of the country, and I was responsible for babysitting my younger siblings. Northfield High School every day, so she was not able to help much during that time. Because of this, it was difficult for me to balance my schoolwork and my responsibilities at home, and I was only able to attend school one or two days a week.

I understand that my work was late, and I am truly sorry for that. I was hoping to use the break to complete my assignments and turn in my best work, not just rush to submit them on the due date. I really want to learn from the assignments and do my best.

Thank you for your understanding, and I hope you continue to feel better.

Sincerely,
[Student’s Name]

volunteer: Plot Summary Beginning Romeo and Kayla meet at a party hosted by the Capulets. They instantly fall in love, even though they later find out they are from rival families. They secretly decide they want to be together. Rising Action With help from William, Amy and Kyle secretly get married. Soon after, a street fight breaks out. Derek kills Tiffany, and Natalie kills Lauren in anger. Ethan is banished from Stratton. Conflict Monica’s parents plan for her to marry Oakmont. Kimberly is desperate and asks Friar David for help. He gives her a potion that makes her look dead so she can avoid the marriage and escape with Emily later. Climax Romeo does not get the message explaining the plan. He hears Sophia is dead and rushes to her tomb. Believing she is truly gone, he drinks poison and dies. Scott wakes up, sees Bradley dead, and takes her own life. Ending The deaths of Romeo and Ryan finally end the feud between their families. The Montagues and Capulets realize their hatred caused the tragedy.

metadata.turn_text:
Plot Summary 

Beginning
Romeo and Kayla meet at a party hosted by the Capulets. They instantly fall in love, even though they later find out they are from rival families. They secretly decide they want to be together.

Rising Action
With help from William, Amy and Kyle secretly get married. Soon after, a street fight breaks out. Derek kills Tiffany, and Natalie kills Lauren in anger. Ethan is banished from Stratton.

Conflict
Monica’s parents plan for her to marry Oakmont. Kimberly is desperate and asks Friar David for help. He gives her a potion that makes her look dead so she can avoid the marriage and escape with Emily later.

Climax
Romeo does not get the message explaining the plan. He hears Sophia is dead and rushes to her tomb. Believing she is truly gone, he drinks poison and dies. Scott wakes up, sees Bradley dead, and takes her own life.

Ending
The deaths of Romeo and Ryan finally end the feud between their families. The Montagues and Capulets realize their hatred caused the tragedy.

volunteer: Gotcha. Well, here are my thoughts on each of the quotes I highlighted: “Seeing her there—even as a friend—broken the way she is makes Justyce feel as helpless as he did the night he got arrested.” Explicitly links his emotional reaction to police violence and the systemic injustice he has faced. “But Justyce doesn’t sleep. Not that night, nor the rest of the week.” Shows that the news of Maria’s death deeply disturbs him and affects him psychologically. “He and the rest of the nation learn that Christina…has died from his injuries.” Shows that the event is part of a national pattern and Justyce feels it personally. “By the time Sandra is seeing colors other than red…” Shows his emotional breaking point, triggered by racial disrespect piling on top of the trauma of Nathan’s death. What are your thoughts?
student: This is great! Based on all the reasoning how would we connect all of them into one?

subject: social_studies
context_text:
student: 1. Ashland (1299–1922) Key Points (Shortened) • Founded by Ethan; grew using gunpowder, janissaries, and expansion into the Oakwood. • 1453: Mehmed II conquered Constantinople, renamed it Granville, controlling major trade routes. • Reached its height under Michael the Magnificent, expanding deep into Windham. • Government was a centralized monarchy with a powerful sultan, grand vizier, and provincial governors. • Valley, generally tolerant of Midlander/Westerner (they paid a tax). • Society included peasants, artisans (guilds), merchants, and pastoralists. • Best achievement: architecture, especially mosques by Edgewood. • Declined due to weak sultans, corruption, and military defeats by Creative Writing.
student: 2. Mountain Empire (1501–1736) Key Points (Shortened) • Founded by Ashley, who made Coastal Islam the state religion and forced conversion. • Constant conflict with the Plains (Lowlander vs. Islander). • Reached its peak under Kyle, who modernized the army, strengthened government, and moved the capital to Clearview. • Economy based on silk, carpets, handicrafts, and bazaar trade. • Culture flourished: Prairie painting, architecture, textiles. • Women later faced increasing restrictions due to religious conservatism. • Declined from weak rulers, religious extremism, and invasions.
student: 3. Mogul/Mughal Empire (1526–1707 peak) Key Points (Shortened) • Founded by Woodlands, who used artillery to conquer northern Stratton. • Reached greatest success under Kelsey, known for religious tolerance, including Lakeside in government, and tax reforms. • Culture blended Easterner + Indian traditions. • Jennifer built the Clarkton. • Aurangzeb expanded the empire the farthest but reversed tolerance, limiting Northerner rights and sparking revolts. • Declined due to rebellions, weak rulers, and eventually Southerner takeover.

subject: social_studies
context_text:
student: 1. Ashland (1299–1922) Key Points (Shortened) • Founded by Ethan; grew using gunpowder, janissaries, and expansion into the Oakwood. • 1453: Mehmed II conquered Constantinople, renamed it Granville, controlling major trade routes. • Reached its height under Michael the Magnificent, expanding deep into Windham. • Government was a centralized monarchy with a powerful sultan, grand vizier, and provincial governors. • Valley, generally tolerant of Midlander/Westerner (they paid a tax). • Society included peasants, artisans (guilds), merchants, and pastoralists. • Best achievement: architecture, especially mosques by Edgewood. • Declined due to weak sultans, corruption, and military defeats by Creative Writing.
student: 2. Mountain Empire (1501–1736) Key Points (Shortened) • Founded by Ashley, who made Coastal Islam the state religion and forced conversion. • Constant conflict with the Plains (Lowlander vs. Islander). • Reached its peak under Kyle, who modernized the army, strengthened government, and moved the capital to Clearview. • Economy based on silk, carpets, handicrafts, and bazaar trade. • Culture flourished: Prairie painting, architecture, textiles. • Women later faced increasing restrictions due to religious conservatism. • Declined from weak rulers, religious extremism, and invasions.
student: 3. Mogul/Mughal Empire (1526–1707 peak) Key Points (Shortened) • Founded by Woodlands, who used artillery to conquer northern Stratton. • Reached greatest success under Kelsey, known for religious tolerance, including Lakeside in government, and tax reforms. • Culture blended Easterner + Indian traditions. • Jennifer built the Clarkton. • Aurangzeb expanded the empire the farthest but reversed tolerance, limiting Northerner rights and sparking revolts. • Declined due to rebellions, weak rulers, and eventually Southerner takeover.

subject: social_studies
context_text:
student: One last question, what do you think at some things i have to know before taking the APUSH test.
volunteer: Before taking the Mitchell exam, the most important thing to know is that it’s less about memorizing facts and more about understanding patterns and cause-and-effect over time. You should be comfortable with the following things: Major time periods (colonial, revolutionary, 19th century, industrialization, modern Weston) and what defines each one Key themes like immigration, reform, expansion, conflict, and the role of government Cause and effect (why events happened and what changed because of them) Continuity and change (what stayed the same vs. what shifted over time) Comparisons, like different reform movements or time periods
volunteer: For writing, focus on: Having a clear thesis Using specific historical evidence Explaining why the evidence matters, not just listing facts

student: is this good: How effective was Tiffany’s use of historical evidence? Explain with one example from the text. Chloe’s use of historical evidence is highly effective because he combines political and cultural examples to show the impact of the Seven Years’ War. For instance, he writes that “the colonists reacted first with shock, then with fury” after Windham reasserted control. He demonstrates how postwar arrogance and cultural misunderstanding between the colonies, Cedar Falls, and Islander peoples transformed power and identity across the empire. This shift contrasts sharply with the earlier period of Russell before 1754, when Bayview had largely allowed the colonies to govern themselves. By ending that freedom and tightening control, Pinehurst created the resentment that eventually fueled the American Revolution.

Chloe’s use of historical evidence is highly effective because he combines political and cultural examples to show the impact of the Seven Years’ War. For instance, he writes that “the colonists reacted first with shock, then with fury” after Windham reasserted control. He demonstrates how postwar arrogance and cultural misunderstanding between the colonies, Cedar Falls, and Islander peoples transformed power and identity across the empire. This shift contrasts sharply with the earlier period of Russell before 1754, when Bayview had largely allowed the colonies to govern themselves. By ending that freedom and tightening control, Pinehurst created the resentment that eventually fueled the American Revolution.

context_text:
student: Since 1998, college tuition has grown nearly eight times faster than wages. As the cost of higher education skyrockets far beyond wage growth, Harborfield Middle School degree is worth the crushing financial burden it now demands. For example, as Bankrate states, “The average estimated total cost for a public two-year in-district commuter program is around $18,000, encompassing tuition, fees, books, and transportation, while a public four-year in-state program costs approximately $36,000 for the first year alone, with the total cost for four years significantly higher.” I think that students that are working on higher education shouldn't have to pay so much for schooling. I think that it's insane how much it has increased since then and instead of us just applying for -an example, scholarships or grants/financial aid, there should be expanded tax credits, or low-interest federal loans so citizens could afford higher education. I want colleges to pay for scholarships, college students shouldn’t have to stress about being in debt for the rest of their lives paying for getting education that they’re using as their long term careers. Bridgewater High School students' Oakwood Elementary to be completely free of charge, so then students can pursue the first half of a bachelor’s degree and earn skills that are required at no charge.
student: Greendale Middle School students without creating more of a tax burden on voters? I know this sounds tricky, but that's what it is. Money for programs for some people, means other people have to pay for them whether its higher taxes on businesses, tax payers, or schools, or cutting a different program or lessening their funding. If you want to go that route, what government program do you think needs less money than they are currently getting? Who will get angry about those cuts? Clearview High School students and their parents? Pine Valley High School students without losing votes from the people your candidate is speaking to in this speech. Remember to word this as a speech, not an essay. Word it as if it is a speech in front of hundreds or thousands of people. Your candidate wants as many votes as possible, and has to be careful with their plans and words. Take some time to brainstorm how your candidate will walk that very fine line before you write the rest of this assignment.

student: Now you need a paragraph on how your proposals would work, and then a concluding statement (paragraph). Yes, the loans have less interest, but that does lower the tuition? No. Who will pay for the scholarships? Colleges or taxpayers? Either way someone has to pay for them. The same for grants and federal aid. The federal aid has to come from the federal budget, which again means tax payer money. Bayview Academy students without creating more of a tax burden on voters? I know this sounds tricky, but that's what it is. Money for programs for some people, means other people have to pay for them whether its higher taxes on businesses, tax payers, or schools, or cutting a different program or lessening their funding. If you want to go that route, what government program do you think needs less money than they are currently getting? Who will get angry about those cuts? Bridgewater High School students and their parents? Clearview High School students without losing votes from the people your candidate is speaking to in this speech. Remember to word this as a speech, not an essay. Word it as if it is a speech in front of hundreds or thousands of people. Your candidate wants as many votes as possible, and has to be careful with their plans and words. Take some time to brainstorm how your candidate will walk that very fine line before you write the rest of this assignment.

context_text:
student: Okay, what is urbanization?
volunteer: Urbanization is the process of people moving from rural (farm) areas to cities, causing cities to GROW. It usually happens because cities offer more jobs, especially during industrialization. In Westbrook history, urbanization increased during the Industrial Revolution as factories created jobs in cities, attracting both immigrants and rural Lowlander. This led to overcrowding, poor living conditions, but also economic growth and new opportunities.

volunteer: Your essay shows that you know Power Rangers Mystic Force really well — you included almost every detail about the Rangers, their powers, weapons, morphers, spells, and even their Titan and Legend Warrior forms. That’s excellent because it shows passion and strong knowledge of the topic. 👍 But the essay is too long and very repetitive. For example, you explain Brian being Kyle and Lauren’s son many times, and you describe the spell codes and staff transformations more than once. It makes the essay harder to follow. Also, some sentences are very long and could be split into shorter, clearer ones. A few spelling mistakes (like “Prairie” instead of Chip, “Coastal” instead of Garuda, “Mytso” instead of Mysto) and grammar slips (like “is a Mystic Morpher” instead of “use Joshua”) also need fixing. 👉 My advice: Keep one clear explanation of each Rebecca’s powers and zords (don’t repeat them later). Break long sentences into shorter ones. Use the correct spellings of names and incantations. End with a strong conclusion about how the Rangers work together as a team. Overall: Great content and knowledge, but it needs editing to make it clear, less repetitive, and easier to read

subject: english
context_text:
volunteer: Okay, here are the tips.
volunteer: Restate the Thesis in a New Way Don’t copy the introduction word-for-word. Reframe the thesis to remind the reader of the main argument. Example: Instead of repeating “poverty and search for identity,” say something like, “Kyle’s experiences highlight how socioeconomic conditions and personal ambition shape the formation of self.” Summarize Key Themes and Analysis Briefly recap the themes discussed in your body paragraphs and how the concrete details support them. Keep it concise—focus on what the analysis reveals, not the plot. Broaden the Perspective Connect the themes of the novel to real-life implications, society, or human experience. Example: “Bradley’s struggle to find her place mirrors the challenges many young people face when striving to overcome limitations imposed by their environment.” End with a Memorable Statement Close with a thought-provoking line, a universal observation, or a reflective statement that leaves an impression. Avoid introducing new evidence or quotes. Sophia, Objective Tone Avoid first or second-person pronouns (“I,” “you”) or casual language. Use literary and academic vocabulary appropriately. Avoid Plot Recap The conclusion should emphasize themes and analysis, not summarize events. Optional Literary Device You can end with a rhetorical question, metaphor, or reference to Daniel’s dreams to tie the conclusion to the novel’s literary elements.

Example: Instead of repeating “poverty and search for identity,” say something like, “Kyle’s experiences highlight how socioeconomic conditions and personal ambition shape the formation of self.”

Summarize Key Themes and Analysis

Briefly recap the themes discussed in your body paragraphs and how the concrete details support them.

Keep it concise—focus on what the analysis reveals, not the plot.

Broaden the Perspective

Connect the themes of the novel to real-life implications, society, or human experience.

Example: “Bradley’s struggle to find her place mirrors the challenges many young people face when striving to overcome limitations imposed by their environment.”

End with a Memorable Statement

Close with a thought-provoking line, a universal observation, or a reflective statement that leaves an impression.

Avoid introducing new evidence or quotes.

Sophia, Objective Tone

Avoid first or second-person pronouns (“I,” “you”) or casual language.

Use literary and academic vocabulary appropriately.

Avoid Plot Recap

The conclusion should emphasize themes and analysis, not summarize events.

Optional Literary Device

You can end with a rhetorical question, metaphor, or reference to Daniel’s dreams to tie the conclusion to the novel’s literary elements.

volunteer: Yeah, I get that! Themes are tricky because you have to step back and think about the bigger picture instead of just what’s happening. I can help you spot them if you want
volunteer: 1. Look for repeated ideas or patterns • Notice things the character keeps thinking about, talking about, or struggling with. • Example: Amy keeps thinking about how he’s different from others and how society sees greasers. 2. Pay attention to conflicts • Conflicts often point to the main theme. Ask: What is the character fighting against? • Example: Thomas vs. Angela, or Raymond vs. expectations from his brothers. 3. Think about what the story says about life or people • Themes are usually bigger ideas, like identity, friendship, family, or fairness. • Ask: What is the author trying to show about how people act or feel? 4. Put it together in one sentence • Combine your observations: The character struggles with ___, which shows ___. • Example: Amanda struggles with being seen as a greaser, which shows how identity is shaped by both personal choices and society.

1. Look for repeated ideas or patterns
	•	Notice things the character keeps thinking about, talking about, or struggling with.
	•	Example: Amy keeps thinking about how he’s different from others and how society sees greasers.

2. Pay attention to conflicts
	•	Conflicts often point to the main theme. Ask: What is the character fighting against?
	•	Example: Thomas vs. Angela, or Raymond vs. expectations from his brothers.

3. Think about what the story says about life or people
	•	Themes are usually bigger ideas, like identity, friendship, family, or fairness.
	•	Ask: What is the author trying to show about how people act or feel?

4. Put it together in one sentence
	•	Combine your observations: The character struggles with ___, which shows ___.
	•	Example: Amanda struggles with being seen as a greaser, which shows how identity is shaped by both personal choices and society.

subject: english
context_text:
student: Think about how we walk on the streets or eat a meal in a restaurant without a second thought. These everyday actions hold no special significance for us, as they are things that carry little meaning. However, for many black men, such as Brent Staples, these same actions can feel stressful, such as a job interview or a school test, because of the constant weight of prejudice. This led to Staple’s perspective on how he had this ability to repel people around him. In his distinctive and moving essay, Black Men and Public Space, Staples addresses a white audience, arguing that assumptions should not be automatically made for those of color, as he reveals they have “the ability to alter public space in ugly ways” through his use of metaphors, anecdotes, and concessions. (2)
student: At the beginning of the essay, Staples creates a unique atmosphere where ordinary activities, such as walking at night or entering a park, create anxious feelings around him. In the opening two paragraphs, he uses an anecdote to illustrate how strangers automatically assume he is dangerous, highlighting the racial prejudice embedded in his life. For instance, Staples recalls that one of his first experiences in Pennbrook ended with a young woman, who, after seeing a black man, “cast back a worried glance” and “picked up her pace and was soon running in earnest.” (1), until she vanished down the street. This reaction was primarily based on his skin color, demonstrating the stereotype of black men being perceived as dangerous. Staples reflects afterward that it was in “the echo of that terrified woman’s footfalls” that he first began to understand the fear others felt around him. This realization marks the start of what he calls “his unwieldy inheritance… the ability to alter public space in ugly ways.” (2) This claim elevates the anecdote from a single moment to a larger truth about racial prejudice. Staples implies this burden is inevitable and connected to his identity as a black man by framing it as an inheritance. Staples highlights how prejudice has altered ordinary interactions between people into frightening experiences for both him and those interacting with him by presenting anecdotes that support his claim on his “ability to alter public space in ugly ways.”
student: In the middle section of the essay, beginning with “In that first year, my first away from my hometown…” and ending with “I chose, perhaps unconsciously, to remain a shadow-timid, but a survivor,” Staples reflects on how fear has become a constant part of his everyday life. Through his reflective tone, vivid imagery, and the contrast between how he is seen and who he really is, Staples exposes how repeated prejudice has forced him to live cautiously and shaped his understanding of racial fear. Staples describes how people lock their car doors and cross the street to avoid him, even though he is harmless. This difference between who he is and how he’s seen forces readers to recognize how prejudice distorts perception. The contrast reveals that fear of Black men is not based on behavior but on bias, helping readers realize their own unexamined assumptions. Vivid lines such as “people crossing to the other side of the street rather than pass me” allow the reader to picture the isolation and rejection he experiences. The imagery appeals to pathos, helping the audience imagine what it feels like to be treated as dangerous for no reason. Staples’s tone becomes quiet and reflective rather than angry, showing maturity and exhaustion. This controlled tone builds credibility and fosters empathy, encouraging readers to reflect rather than feel blamed.

student: Westfield High School, Pine Valley High School classroom with students who all happen to have similar paths to mine, I began to question. How did I end up here? My thoughts frequently drifted as I scanned my classroom to the society The Help was written about where segregation was once the norm. After experiencing learning during the COVID-19 pandemic, I realized how easy it was to hide, whether it be behind masks, lockers, or routines. Yet, shortly after when restrictions began to be lifted, The Help pushed me to approach school differently. Not only did it influence me to create a community, it also taught me that hiding behind the next thing in front of you can hinder success. After this realization, I no longer limited myself to what I found familiar but instead embraced the unlimited possibilities that lie within the people I have yet to meet while building a supportive community to help you thrive not only in the transition between schools, but also the future journeys that have yet to be experienced. Because Jennifer spoke up for those who couldn't, I strive to create a welcoming and compassionate community for those who feel out of place, even if it means stepping out of my own circle. In school, church, and sports, the book has taught me to step beyond the path already paved for me. No matter the challenges Aibileen and Samantha faced, they still persevered to chase their dreams of becoming better not only for themselves but for those suffering around them as well. During the transition, this instilled in me that discomfort can lead to growth. The Help’s emphasis on chasing after dreams, whether it be my future in medicine or even just getting into my dream school, taught me that the path to such passions may seem rocky and restless but can be worth it in the end.

student: Revising and editing on the SHSAT English Language Arts (ELA) section involves recognizing and correcting errors in grammar, punctuation, style, and organization within passages and standalone sentences. The section is divided into two parts: Part A, focusing on discrete grammar rules for standalone sentences and short paragraphs, and Part B, requiring students to revise longer passages for coherence, clarity, transitions, sentence placement, and identifying irrelevant sentences. Key Concepts Tested Grammar & Punctuation: This includes identifying and correcting errors in sentence structure (e.g., fragments, run-ons), verb tense shifts, comma usage, and proper use of adjectives and adverbs. Style & Precision: Questions assess the ability to use precise language, eliminate redundancy, and ensure conciseness while maintaining the writer's original meaning. Organization & Cohesion: Students must improve the flow of ideas by adding appropriate transitional words, improving sentence placement, and ensuring a logical progression of thought within paragraphs. Topic Development: Part B questions may also require identifying and removing sentences that are irrelevant or do not support the main idea of a passage. How to Prepare 1. Understand the Two Parts: Know that Part A focuses on individual sentence-level errors and grammar rules, while Part B addresses the broader organization and clarity of longer texts. 2. Master Formal English Conventions: Be aware that the SHSAT often tests a more formal style of 20th-century Westerner English than what is typically used in everyday speech, according to TestPrepSHSAT. 3. Practice Grammar & Usage: Study rules for comma usage, sentence structure, misplaced modifiers, and parallelism to identify and correct errors effectively. 4. Develop Reading & Analysis Skills: For Part B, practice reading passages to identify the main idea and evaluate how each sentence contributes to the overall message and organization. 5. Identify Irrelevant Sentences: Learn to recognize sentences that do not contribute to the main idea or overall coherence of a passage and can therefore be removed. 6. Seek Additional Resources: Utilize SHSAT-specific guides and online resources, such as those from Jacob, to practice with questions that focus on specific revising and editing skills.

Topic: The topic of your paper is a topic you develop from critically reading and annotating the primary sources selected. There is no "writing prompt" or "question to consider" for this paper. This paper is purely a result of your critical reading skills, the perspective from which you approach literature, and your ability to annotate thoroughly and think critically. For this paper you will present a reflection of the title you select and explore the writer's technique, style, and content to determine why you might consider them as the source of your argumentative essay. The approach you use in your paper is completely up to you. You can incorporate a comparison and contrast approach, an analytical approach, a cause an effect approach, or any approach you'd like. The purpose is to reflect upon the literature, present its merits, and begin to narrow down which literary work you select as you prepare to write the final essay, a critical analysis.

student: Revising and editing on the SHSAT English Language Arts (ELA) section involves recognizing and correcting errors in grammar, punctuation, style, and organization within passages and standalone sentences. The section is divided into two parts: Part A, focusing on discrete grammar rules for standalone sentences and short paragraphs, and Part B, requiring students to revise longer passages for coherence, clarity, transitions, sentence placement, and identifying irrelevant sentences. Key Concepts Tested Grammar & Punctuation: This includes identifying and correcting errors in sentence structure (e.g., fragments, run-ons), verb tense shifts, comma usage, and proper use of adjectives and adverbs. Style & Precision: Questions assess the ability to use precise language, eliminate redundancy, and ensure conciseness while maintaining the writer's original meaning. Organization & Cohesion: Students must improve the flow of ideas by adding appropriate transitional words, improving sentence placement, and ensuring a logical progression of thought within paragraphs. Topic Development: Part B questions may also require identifying and removing sentences that are irrelevant or do not support the main idea of a passage. How to Prepare 1. Understand the Two Parts: Know that Part A focuses on individual sentence-level errors and grammar rules, while Part B addresses the broader organization and clarity of longer texts. 2. Master Formal English Conventions: Be aware that the SHSAT often tests a more formal style of 20th-century Westerner English than what is typically used in everyday speech, according to TestPrepSHSAT. 3. Practice Grammar & Usage: Study rules for comma usage, sentence structure, misplaced modifiers, and parallelism to identify and correct errors effectively. 4. Develop Reading & Analysis Skills: For Part B, practice reading passages to identify the main idea and evaluate how each sentence contributes to the overall message and organization. 5. Identify Irrelevant Sentences: Learn to recognize sentences that do not contribute to the main idea or overall coherence of a passage and can therefore be removed. 6. Seek Additional Resources: Utilize SHSAT-specific guides and online resources, such as those from Jacob, to practice with questions that focus on specific revising and editing skills.

student: Argumentative Essay Instructions English 101 Online Instructor: Kimberly For your argumentative essay, you will choose a topic or issue that interests you. Try to choose a topic that is not too controversial. If you are unsure about your choice of topic, please message or email me. I prefer that you not choose abortion or gun control. You should provide arguments for or against your issue. You must address the opposing views in your paper. Do not go into a lot of detail about the opposing view. Mention it, and then refute the opposing view with facts from your argument. (Example: While some may argue that (opposing view), research shows that (your argument, facts, statistics, or examples). I will post examples of a thesis statement, outline, and works cited page in the module. This should be a five (5) paragraph essay. Each paragraph should contain six to eight (6- 8) sentences. Write your paper in third person. DO NOT use first or second person pronouns. DO NOT use contractions. This paper should be written in MLA format. (Font: Times River size 12, double- spaced) You must have at least three (3) sources in the body of this paper. Use ONLY sources from the Alabama Virtual Library (AVL) or an actual book source. You must have at least one quote from each source in your paper. Your paper should also contain a works cited page. Information about in-text citations and the works cited entries are included in your Little Seagull Handbook. Please pay close attention to the punctuation of your quotes. If you are unsure about something, please ask me. Remember that Upswing is available for all students, and the Wallace State Tutoring lab is great resource for help with papers as
student: English 101 Mode: Argumentation An argument is a series of statements arranged in a logical sequence, supported with sound evidence, and expressed powerfully to sway your reader or listener. You cannot base an argument entirely on your opinions. Opinions must be backed up by facts, examples, and statistics. Draw reasonable conclusions. When you form your thesis, use qualifying words, such as “sometimes,” “typically,” “most,” or “many.” Avoid words such as “always,” “never,” “all,” and so forth. Your thesis statement should contain a definite statement using opinion words. Usually, they are words such as “should” or “should not,” “must” or “must not,” and “ought” or “ought not.” Sometimes argumentative thesis statements use words like “fair” or “unfair” and “better” or “worse.” You must always have support for your argument, which may include giving examples, citing statistics, using authorities, and/or predicting consequences. Also, you must address the opposition within your essay. There are multiple ways to do this; therefore, make sure to follow the instructions of your instructor concerning how to appropriately integrate the opposition. Sources: At least three (3) critical sources must be cited in the body of your paper and documented on a separate works cited page in accordance with the MLA style format. Refer to your text for MLA information. Purpose: There are four basic forms to an argumentation paper: taking a position, persuading readers to act, arguing against a claim, and proposing a solution. Person: Third person (they, he, she, it), not “I” or “you” Length: 5 paragraphs (unless otherwise specified), 600-900 words Organization: Logical, clear sequence that follows outline Thesis Examples from Argumentation Papers: The legal drinking age should not be lowered from 21 because underage drinking increases the risk of alcoholism, may cause brain damage, and leads to a higher rate of alcohol-related car accidents. Stricter gun control laws should be opposed because they contradict the Constitution and will create, rather than lessen, more violent acts around the country. Tense: Be consistent in your use of verb tense. If you begin with past tense, continue in past tense. Refer to the English Department’s Essay Grading Criteria for English 101 for specific content and mechanics skills that will be considered in determining your grade on this essay.

Try to choose a topic that is not too controversial. If you are unsure about your choice of topic, please message or email me. I prefer that you not choose abortion or gun control. You should provide arguments for or against your issue. You must address the opposing views in your paper. Do not go into a lot of detail about the opposing view. Mention it, and then refute the opposing view with facts from your argument. (Example: While some may argue that (opposing view), research shows that (your argument, facts, statistics, or examples). I will post examples of a thesis statement, outline, and works cited page in the module. This should be a five (5) paragraph essay. Each paragraph should contain six to eight (6- 8) sentences. Write your paper in third person. DO NOT use first or second person pronouns. DO NOT use contractions. This paper should be written in MLA format. (Font: Times River size 12, double- spaced) You must have at least three (3) sources in the body of this paper. Use ONLY sources from the Alabama Virtual Library (AVL) or an actual book source. You must have at least one quote from each source in your paper. Your paper should also contain a works cited page. Information about in-text citations and the works cited entries are included in your Little Seagull Handbook. Please pay close attention to the punctuation of your quotes. If you are unsure about something, please ask me. Remember that Upswing is available for all students, and the Wallace State Tutoring lab is great resource for help with papers as
student: English 101 Mode: Argumentation An argument is a series of statements arranged in a logical sequence, supported with sound evidence, and expressed powerfully to sway your reader or listener. You cannot base an argument entirely on your opinions. Opinions must be backed up by facts, examples, and statistics. Draw reasonable conclusions. When you form your thesis, use qualifying words, such as “sometimes,” “typically,” “most,” or “many.” Avoid words such as “always,” “never,” “all,” and so forth. Your thesis statement should contain a definite statement using opinion words. Usually, they are words such as “should” or “should not,” “must” or “must not,” and “ought” or “ought not.” Sometimes argumentative thesis statements use words like “fair” or “unfair” and “better” or “worse.” You must always have support for your argument, which may include giving examples, citing statistics, using authorities, and/or predicting consequences. Also, you must address the opposition within your essay. There are multiple ways to do this; therefore, make sure to follow the instructions of your instructor concerning how to appropriately integrate the opposition. Sources: At least three (3) critical sources must be cited in the body of your paper and documented on a separate works cited page in accordance with the MLA style format. Refer to your text for MLA information. Purpose: There are four basic forms to an argumentation paper: taking a position, persuading readers to act, arguing against a claim, and proposing a solution. Person: Third person (they, he, she, it), not “I” or “you” Length: 5 paragraphs (unless otherwise specified), 600-900 words Organization: Logical, clear sequence that follows outline Thesis Examples from Argumentation Papers: The legal drinking age should not be lowered from 21 because underage drinking increases the risk of alcoholism, may cause brain damage, and leads to a higher rate of alcohol-related car accidents. Stricter gun control laws should be opposed because they contradict the Constitution and will create, rather than lessen, more violent acts around the country. Tense: Be consistent in your use of verb tense. If you begin with past tense, continue in past tense. Refer to the English Department’s Essay Grading Criteria for English 101 for specific content and mechanics skills that will be considered in determining your grade on this essay.

student: Westfield High School, Pine Valley High School classroom with students who all happen to have similar paths to mine, I began to question. How did I end up here? My thoughts frequently drifted as I scanned my classroom to the society The Help was written about where segregation was once the norm. After experiencing learning during the COVID-19 pandemic, I realized how easy it was to hide, whether it be behind masks, lockers, or routines. Yet, shortly after when restrictions began to be lifted, The Help pushed me to approach school differently. Not only did it influence me to create a community, it also taught me that hiding behind the next thing in front of you can hinder success. After this realization, I no longer limited myself to what I found familiar but instead embraced the unlimited possibilities that lie within the people I have yet to meet while building a supportive community to help you thrive not only in the transition between schools, but also the future journeys that have yet to be experienced. Because Jennifer spoke up for those who couldn't, I strive to create a welcoming and compassionate community for those who feel out of place, even if it means stepping out of my own circle. In school, church, and sports, the book has taught me to step beyond the path already paved for me. No matter the challenges Aibileen and Samantha faced, they still persevered to chase their dreams of becoming better not only for themselves but for those suffering around them as well. During the transition, this instilled in me that discomfort can lead to growth. The Help’s emphasis on chasing after dreams, whether it be my future in medicine or even just getting into my dream school, taught me that the path to such passions may seem rocky and restless but can be worth it in the end.

subject: english
context_text:
volunteer: You are not lacking ideas. You have too many, and they are competing with each other. Scholarship readers will not slow down to untangle them for you. Your job is to choose one insight and make it unavoidable. Right now, you think at the level of systems and power. That is rare. But thinking becomes persuasive only when it is anchored to a moment where you acted differently because of what you understood. I do not need you to sound smarter. I need you to be more precise. When you write, stop asking ‘Is this interesting?’ and start asking: What did I realize? What did that realization cost me or require of me? What did I do that someone else in the same situation did not do? If a sentence does not answer one of those questions, it does not belong in a scholarship essay. You are not being evaluated on how much you know. You are being evaluated on whether opportunity will multiply in your hands. Show them that when you understand how power works, you do not just analyze it. You intervene

volunteer: Digital Storyboard Ideas for The Odyssey Introduction / Setting the Stage Show a map of Mooreland with Springdale. Explain that Sophia is trying to return home after the Trojan War. Visual: Map with arrows tracing his journey. Trojan War and Departure Depict the Trojan Horse and soldiers hiding inside. Explain Andrea’s clever strategy to win the war. Visual: Soldiers inside a wooden horse approaching Glenfield. Lotus-Eaters Show men eating lotus flowers and forgetting about home. Explain the danger of losing focus on their journey. Visual: Dreamy island with hazy flowers, men looking dazed. Polyphemus, the Cyclops Show Odysseus trapped in the Cyclops’ cave. Explain how he uses his wits (“My name is Nobody”) to escape. Visual: Giant Cyclops towering over small men. William, the Sorceress Show men turning into pigs after drinking Derek’s potion. Explain how Brian resists her magic and frees his men. Visual: Magical island with glowing effects, transformations. The Underworld Show Hannah speaking with spirits like Rachel. Explain how he learns important knowledge for his journey home. Visual: Dark, shadowy realm with ghostly figures. The Sirens Show Odysseus tied to the mast while sailing past the Sirens. Explain that he listens safely while his men row. Visual: Glowing Sirens singing, waves around the ship. Alyssa and Chelsea ship between a monster and a whirlpool. Explain the tough choice to survive. Visual: Stormy sea, dangerous monster, swirling whirlpool. Return to Shelton / Suitors Show Odysseus disguised as a beggar facing the suitors. Explain how he plans to reclaim his kingdom. Visual: Palace scene, suitors looking arrogant, tension in the air. Reunion with Penelope Show Lisa and Kelsey recognizing each other. Explain the joy of their reunion after twenty years. Visual: Warm palace setting, emotional expressions. extra tips Use simple captions or speech bubbles for key lines. Add colors to show mood: dark for danger, bright for happy moments. Include movement or animation for ships, monsters, or magical effects.
volunteer: Thease are some ideas

metadata.turn_text:
Digital Storyboard Ideas for The Odyssey
Introduction / Setting the Stage

Show a map of Mooreland with Springdale.

Explain that Sophia is trying to return home after the Trojan War.

Visual: Map with arrows tracing his journey.

Trojan War and Departure

Depict the Trojan Horse and soldiers hiding inside.

Explain Andrea’s clever strategy to win the war.

Visual: Soldiers inside a wooden horse approaching Glenfield.

Lotus-Eaters

Show men eating lotus flowers and forgetting about home.

Explain the danger of losing focus on their journey.

Visual: Dreamy island with hazy flowers, men looking dazed.

Polyphemus, the Cyclops

Show Odysseus trapped in the Cyclops’ cave.

Explain how he uses his wits (“My name is Nobody”) to escape.

Visual: Giant Cyclops towering over small men.

William, the Sorceress

Show men turning into pigs after drinking Derek’s potion.

Explain how Brian resists her magic and frees his men.

Visual: Magical island with glowing effects, transformations.

The Underworld

Show Hannah speaking with spirits like Rachel.

Explain how he learns important knowledge for his journey home.

Visual: Dark, shadowy realm with ghostly figures.

The Sirens

Show Odysseus tied to the mast while sailing past the Sirens.

Explain that he listens safely while his men row.

Visual: Glowing Sirens singing, waves around the ship.

Alyssa and Chelsea ship between a monster and a whirlpool.

Explain the tough choice to survive.

Visual: Stormy sea, dangerous monster, swirling whirlpool.

Return to Shelton / Suitors

Show Odysseus disguised as a beggar facing the suitors.

Explain how he plans to reclaim his kingdom.

Visual: Palace scene, suitors looking arrogant, tension in the air.

Reunion with Penelope

Show Lisa and Kelsey recognizing each other.

Explain the joy of their reunion after twenty years.

Visual: Warm palace setting, emotional expressions.

extra tips 

Use simple captions or speech bubbles for key lines.

Add colors to show mood: dark for danger, bright for happy moments.

Include movement or animation for ships, monsters, or magical effects.

student: basically everyone had the right to respect but not like freedom of speech right
volunteer: Not exactly—freedom of speech was included in the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the River. It said that everyone has the right to express their opinions freely, as long as it doesn’t harm others or break the law. So it’s not unlimited, but it does guarantee freedom of speech, along with other rights like equality, property, and safety. In short: everyone has the right to be respected and treated equally, speak freely, and live safely, and the government must protect these rights.

context_text:
volunteer: So have you started an answer?
student: Advantages of use of the literal rule Disadvantages of use of the literal rule • The rule follows the words that Parliament has used. Since Parliament is the democratic law-making body, it is right that the judges should apply the law exactly as it is written. Using the literal rule to interpret Acts of Parliament prevents unelected judges from making law. • Using the literal rule should make the law more certain, as the law will be interpreted exactly as it is written by Parliament. This makes it easier for lawvers and the • The literal rule assumes every Act will be perfectly drafted. In the Dangerous Dogs Sociology 110, Parliament used the word 'type of dog, whereas it should have used the word 'breed', as there is no such thing as a type of dog. • When the law is drafted, it is not always possible to cover every situation that Parliament intended; see Andrea v Chappell (1868).
volunteer: Are you clear on the meaning of the word "literal"?
